subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What analog IC design is and why it still matters in an all-digital world.
keyPoints: Every digital chip needs analog: power management, clocking, I/O drivers
ADC/DAC: bridge between analog real world and digital processing
PLL: generates stable clock frequencies from a reference
LDO: provides clean, regulated supply voltage to sensitive circuits
Analog design requires: SPICE simulation, layout, mismatch awareness
CMOS analog: uses MOSFETs in saturation as amplifying devices
Key challenge: process/voltage/temperature (PVT) variation
subLevels.content: Analog IC design deals with continuous signals — voltages and currents that can take any value, not just 0 or 1. Every modern SoC has significant analog content: power management ICs regulate supply voltages, PLLs generate clocks, ADCs digitize sensor inputs, and I/O drivers interface with the outside world. Analog design requires deep understanding of MOSFET physics, circuit theory, and the ability to think about noise, mismatch, and PVT variation simultaneously.

subLevels.title: Key Concepts
subLevels.summary: Essential analog IC terminology and fundamental relationships.
keyPoints: gm: transconductance — how much drain current changes per gate voltage change
ro: output resistance — models channel length modulation
Av: voltage gain = -gm × ro (for common-source amplifier)
VGS: gate-to-source voltage — controls drain current
VDS: drain-to-source voltage — must exceed VGS-VT for saturation
VT: threshold voltage — gate voltage to turn on the transistor
ID = (1/2) × μCox × (W/L) × (VGS - VT)²  (saturation)

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through MOSFET operating regions and identify saturation.
keyPoints: Cutoff: VGS < VT → ID ≈ 0 (transistor OFF)
Linear/Triode: VDS < VGS - VT → ID depends on both VGS and VDS
Saturation: VDS ≥ VGS - VT → ID depends mainly on VGS
Analog circuits operate in saturation — current source behavior
Digital circuits switch between cutoff and triode
Overdrive voltage: VOD = VGS - VT (key design parameter)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Calculate MOSFET operating point and small-signal parameters.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 0: MOSFET Operating Point Analysis

Given NMOS transistor parameters:
- VT = 0.4V
- μCox × W/L = 2 mA/V²
- λ = 0.1 V⁻¹
- VGS = 0.8V
- VDS = 1.0V

**Solve:**
1. Determine the operating region (show comparison of VDS vs VOD)
2. Calculate drain current ID
3. Calculate transconductance gm
4. Calculate output resistance ro
5. Calculate intrinsic voltage gain |Av| = gm × ro
6. Is this transistor suitable as an amplifier? Why?

**Show all work with units.**
lab.solutionExplanation: VOD=0.4V, VDS=1.0V>VOD → Saturation. ID=160μA. gm=0.8mA/V. ro=62.5kΩ. Av=50V/V. This transistor is in saturation and suitable as an amplifier — it has significant gain and operates as a controlled current source.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your analog IC fundamentals.

quiz.q: For analog amplification, MOSFET must be in:
Transconductance gm represents:
options: Change in drain current per change in gate voltage

quiz.q: Channel length modulation parameter λ affects:
Overdrive voltage VOD is:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Circuit fundamentals — KCL, KVL, RC filters, and frequency response.
keyPoints: KCL: sum of currents entering a node = sum leaving (∑I = 0)
KVL: sum of voltages around any loop = 0 (∑V = 0)
RC low-pass filter: passes low frequencies, attenuates high
Pole frequency: f_p = 1/(2π×RC) — -3dB point
Transfer function: H(s) = Vout/Vin expressed in s-domain
Bode plot: magnitude (dB) and phase vs log frequency
-20dB/decade slope after each pole
subLevels.content: KCL and KVL are the foundation of all circuit analysis. RC filters are the simplest frequency-selective networks — an RC low-pass filter has H(jω) = 1/(1+jωRC). At ω=1/RC (the pole), gain drops by 3dB (0.707×) and phase is -45°. Above this frequency, gain falls at -20dB/decade. Every analog circuit can be analyzed as a network of such poles and zeros.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: RC filter, transfer functions, and impedance calculations.
keyPoints: Capacitor impedance: ZC = 1/(jωC) = 1/(sC)
Inductor impedance: ZL = jωL = sL
RC low-pass: H(s) = 1/(1 + sRC) = ωp/(s + ωp)
Pole frequency: ωp = 1/RC → fp = 1/(2πRC)
Gain at DC (s=0): H(0) = 1 (unity for RC LPF)
Gain at pole: |H(jωp)| = 1/√2 = -3.01 dB
Phase at pole: ∠H(jωp) = -45°

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Derive the transfer function and Bode plot for an RC high-pass filter.
keyPoints: RC HPF: C in series, R to ground
H(s) = R/(R + 1/sC) = sRC/(1 + sRC)
Zero at s=0 (DC is blocked)
Pole at s=-1/RC (same as LPF)
At high frequency: H→1 (unity gain)
Slope: +20dB/decade below pole

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Design an RC low-pass filter and calculate its frequency response.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 1: RC Filter Design

Design a low-pass RC filter for an anti-aliasing application:
- Required -3dB frequency: 100 kHz
- Available capacitor: C = 10 pF

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate required R for fp = 100 kHz
2. Write the transfer function H(s)
3. Calculate |H| (in dB) at: 10kHz, 100kHz, 1MHz, 10MHz
4. Calculate phase ∠H at the same frequencies
5. How many decades above fp is the 1MHz point? What is the expected attenuation?

**Show all calculations with units.**
lab.solutionExplanation: R=159kΩ gives fp=100kHz. At 1MHz (10×fp=1 decade above): attenuation=-20dB. At 10MHz (100×fp=2 decades): -40dB. Phase at 1MHz≈-84°. The -20dB/decade roll-off above the pole is the key characteristic of a single-pole RC filter.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your circuit fundamentals.

quiz.q: RC low-pass filter pole frequency is:
At the pole frequency, gain drops by:
Above the pole, RC LPF rolls off at:
options: Both B and C are correct

quiz.q: Phase of RC LPF at the pole frequency:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: MOSFET deep dive — IV characteristics, small-signal model, and body effect.
keyPoints: NMOS: threshold voltage VTN > 0, enhanced by positive VGS
PMOS: threshold voltage VTP < 0, enhanced by negative VGS
Body effect: VSB increases VT → VT = VT0 + γ(√(2φF+VSB) - √(2φF))
Channel length modulation: ID ≈ (μCox/2)(W/L)(VGS-VT)²(1+λVDS)
Short-channel effects: below 100nm, simple square law breaks down
Small-signal model: gmVgs current source + ro + Cgs + Cgd
Unity gain frequency: fT = gm/(2π(Cgs+Cgd))
subLevels.content: The MOSFET small-signal model is essential for amplifier analysis. Replace the MOSFET with: a voltage-controlled current source gmVgs (between drain and source), output resistance ro (drain to source), gate capacitance Cgs, and feedback capacitance Cgd (Miller effect). Body effect increases threshold voltage when source is not at the same potential as bulk — common in CMOS analog circuits where transistors are stacked.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: MOSFET model equations, body effect, and unity gain frequency.
keyPoints: ID(sat) = (μnCox/2)(W/L)(VGS-VT)²(1+λVDS)
gm = √(2μnCox(W/L)ID) = 2ID/(VGS-VT)
gbs = gmb = gm × η where η = γ/(2√(2φF+VSB)) ≈ 0.1-0.2
fT = gm/(2π × Cgs) (ignoring Cgd for simplicity)
Cgs ≈ (2/3)WLCox + WCov (gate-source capacitance)
Miller effect: Cgd appears as Cgd(1+Av) at input
gm ∝ √(W/L × ID) — increase W/L or ID to increase gm

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Draw and analyze the complete small-signal model of a common-source amplifier.
keyPoints: CS amplifier: input at gate, output at drain, source grounded
Small-signal: replace MOSFET with gmVgs + ro
Load resistor RD in parallel with ro
Av = -gm × (ro || RD)
Input resistance: Rin = ∞ (gate is insulated)
Output resistance: Rout = ro || RD

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Analyze a common-source amplifier — find gain, bandwidth, and fT.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 2: Common-Source Amplifier Analysis

NMOS CS amplifier with:
- μnCox = 200 μA/V², W/L = 20
- VT = 0.5V, λ = 0.05 V⁻¹
- Bias: ID = 200 μA
- RD = 20 kΩ
- Cgs = 50 fF, Cgd = 10 fF

**Solve:**
1. Find VGS for the given ID (use ID = (μnCox/2)(W/L)(VGS-VT)²)
2. Calculate gm and ro
3. Calculate midband voltage gain Av = -gm(ro||RD)
4. Miller capacitance at input: CM = Cgd(1+|Av|)
5. Input pole frequency: fin = 1/(2πRsig×Cin) where Rsig=1kΩ, Cin=Cgs+CM
6. Unity gain frequency fT
lab.solutionExplanation: VOD=0.316V, gm=1.265mA/V, ro=100kΩ. Gain Av≈-21V/V. Miller effect multiplies Cgd by (1+21)=22× at the input, creating dominant input pole. This Miller multiplication is why cascode amplifiers are used in high-frequency designs — cascode eliminates Miller effect.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your MOSFET deep knowledge.

quiz.q: Body effect increases threshold voltage when:
Miller effect means Cgd appears at input as:
To increase gm while keeping ID constant:
Unity gain frequency fT is limited by:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Current mirrors — the fundamental analog building block for biasing.
keyPoints: Current mirror copies a reference current to one or more outputs
Basic mirror: diode-connected reference + output transistor
Accuracy depends on VDS matching — basic mirror has finite ro error
Cascode mirror: stacks transistors to increase output impedance
Wilson mirror: feedback improves accuracy significantly
Current ratio: set by W/L ratios (Iout = Iref × (W/L)out/(W/L)ref)
Output impedance: basic=ro, cascode=gm×ro², Wilson≈gm×ro²
subLevels.content: Current mirrors are used everywhere in analog ICs — they bias amplifiers, set operating points, and implement active loads. The basic NMOS mirror: the reference transistor has its gate connected to its drain (diode-connected), fixing VGS=VGS_ref. The output transistor shares the same VGS, so it carries the same current (if same W/L). The error comes from VDS mismatch — the diode-connected transistor has VDS=VGS, but the output transistor may have different VDS, causing different ID due to channel length modulation.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: Current mirror ratio, output impedance, and systematic error.
keyPoints: Basic mirror: Iout = Iref × (W/L)out/(W/L)ref × (1+λ×ΔVDS)
Systematic error: ΔI/I = λ×(VDS_out - VDS_ref)
Basic mirror Rout = ro = 1/(λ×Iout)
Cascode mirror Rout = gm×ro × ro ≈ gm×ro²
Minimum output voltage: Vmin = VOD (basic), 2×VOD (cascode)
High-swing cascode: Vmin = VOD + VDS_sat ≈ 2×VOD
Current accuracy improves with longer L (smaller λ)

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a cascode current mirror with specified output impedance.
keyPoints: Cascode stacks M3 on M1 (reference) and M4 on M2 (output)
M3 and M4 gates biased at VGS + 2×VOD from supply
Cascode improves Rout from ro to gm×ro²
Minimum output voltage = 2×VOD (two saturation stacks)
Used when high output impedance is needed (current source for diff pair)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Design a current mirror with specific current ratio and calculate accuracy.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 3: Current Mirror Design

Design a PMOS current mirror to source 500μA from a 100μA reference.

Parameters:
- μpCox = 100 μA/V², |VTP| = 0.5V, λp = 0.08 V⁻¹
- Reference transistor M1: W/L = 10
- VDD = 1.8V

**Tasks:**
1. Find W/L of output transistor M2 for Iout = 500μA
2. Calculate |VOD| for M1 (using Iref=100μA)
3. Calculate |VGS| = |VTP| + |VOD|
4. Calculate output impedance ro of M2
5. If VSD_out = 0.8V vs VSD_ref = |VGS|, find systematic error %
6. What minimum W/L ratio would halve the error (use longer L)?

**Show all work.**
hints: Error = 0.08×(0.8-0.947)×100 = -1.18% (Iout is slightly less than ideal)
lab.solutionExplanation: WL_M2=50 for 5:1 ratio. VOD=0.447V for M1. ro of M2=25kΩ. The systematic error is -1.18% because VSD_out < VSD_ref. To reduce error: use longer channel length (reduces λ), use cascode mirror, or use Wilson mirror with feedback.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your current mirror knowledge.

quiz.q: In a basic current mirror, the reference transistor is:
Current ratio in a mirror is set by:
Cascode mirror has higher output impedance because:
Minimum output voltage of cascode mirror:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Differential pair — the core of every op-amp and comparator.
keyPoints: Diff pair: two matched MOSFETs with shared tail current source
Differential mode: Vdiff = V1 - V2 — amplified
Common mode: Vcm = (V1+V2)/2 — rejected
CMRR: Common Mode Rejection Ratio = Adiff/Acm (want high)
Differential gain: Ad = gm × RD (with resistive load)
Common-mode gain: Acm = -RD/(2×RSS) (RSS = tail source impedance)
CMRR = Ad/Acm = gm × RSS (higher tail impedance → better CMRR)
subLevels.content: The differential pair is the most important analog building block — it's the input stage of every op-amp, comparator, and sense amplifier. Two matched transistors share a tail current source. When a differential signal is applied, one transistor turns on more while the other turns on less — the difference is amplified. Common-mode signals (both inputs move together) are rejected because the tail current source is a high impedance — it doesn't let the total current change.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: Differential pair gain, CMRR, and input range calculations.
keyPoints: Tail current: ISS splits equally → ID = ISS/2 per transistor
gm = √(2μnCox(W/L)(ISS/2)) = √(μnCox(W/L)ISS)
Differential gain: Ad = gm × RD (single-ended output)
Common-mode gain: Acm ≈ -1/(2gm_tail×RSS)
CMRR (dB) = 20log|Ad/Acm| = 20log(2gm×RSS)
Linear input range: |Vid| < √(ISS/(μnCox×W/L))
Active load (current mirror): doubles differential gain to gm×ro

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Analyze a differential pair with active load (current mirror).
keyPoints: Active load: PMOS current mirror replaces resistors
One mirror output combines with other diff pair output
Differential gain doubles: Ad = gm × ro_total
ro_total = ron || rop (NMOS and PMOS output resistances)
Ad = gm × (ron || rop) — single-ended output
This is the core of a two-stage op-amp first stage

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Design a differential pair with active load meeting gain and CMRR specs.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 4: Differential Pair Design

Design an NMOS differential pair with PMOS active load:
- Supply: VDD = 1.8V
- Tail current: ISS = 400μA
- μnCox = 200μA/V², μpCox = 100μA/V²
- λn = 0.05V⁻¹, λp = 0.08V⁻¹
- Required: |Ad| ≥ 20 V/V

**Tasks:**
1. Choose W/L for NMOS pair (target VOD = 0.2V)
2. Calculate gm for NMOS transistors
3. Calculate ron (NMOS) and rop (PMOS) (use same ID=ISS/2)
   - For PMOS, use same W/L as NMOS scaled by μn/μp
4. Calculate Ad = gm × (ron || rop)
5. Verify Ad ≥ 20 V/V
6. Calculate CMRR if tail source Rss = 200kΩ
lab.solutionExplanation: WL_n=50 gives VOD=0.2V. gm=2mA/V. ron=100kΩ, rop=62.5kΩ. Rout=38.5kΩ. Ad=77V/V >> 20V/V target. CMRR=58dB. The active load (current mirror) enables much higher gain than resistive load by presenting high impedance.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your differential pair knowledge.

quiz.q: Why does a differential pair reject common-mode signals?
options: High-impedance tail source prevents total current change

quiz.q: CMRR improves when tail source impedance:
Active load (current mirror) replaces resistors to:
options: Double the differential gain by combining both sides

quiz.q: Each transistor in the diff pair carries:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Two-stage CMOS op-amp — the most important analog circuit.
keyPoints: Stage 1: Differential pair with active load → high gain, differential to single-ended
Stage 2: Common-source amplifier → additional gain and output drive
Total gain: A = A1 × A2 = (gm1×Rout1) × (gm2×Rout2)
Compensation: Miller capacitor Cc between stages prevents oscillation
GBW: Gain-Bandwidth product = gm1/(2π×Cc)
Phase margin: must be > 45° (typically design for 60°)
Slew rate: SR = ISS/Cc (how fast output can swing)
subLevels.content: The two-stage CMOS op-amp consists of a differential input pair (stage 1) followed by a common-source amplifier (stage 2). Stage 1 converts the differential input to a single-ended signal with gain A1=gm1×Rout1. Stage 2 provides additional gain A2=gm6×Rout2. The total open-loop gain is typically 60-80dB. A Miller compensation capacitor Cc between the gate and drain of the second stage ensures stability — it creates a dominant pole that keeps phase margin positive when the loop is closed.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: Two-stage op-amp design equations — gain, GBW, phase margin, slew rate.
keyPoints: First stage gain: A1 = -gm1 × (ro2 || ro4)
Second stage gain: A2 = -gm6 × (ro6 || ro7)
Total DC gain: A0 = A1 × A2
Dominant pole: ω_p1 = 1/(Cc × gm6 × Rout2) (Miller effect)
GBW = gm1 / (2π × Cc)
Second pole: ω_p2 = gm6 / CL (load capacitance pole)
Phase margin ≈ 90° - arctan(GBW/fp2)

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a complete two-stage op-amp meeting spec — step by step.
keyPoints: Spec: GBW=10MHz, PM=60°, CL=5pF, SR≥20V/μs
Step 1: Choose Cc = 3pF (from CL/1.67)
Step 2: gm1 = GBW×2π×Cc = 188μA/V
Step 3: ISS from SR: ISS=SR×Cc=60μA (ISS/2=30μA each)
Step 4: Verify gm1 is achievable with ISS/2
Step 5: Size M6 for gm6 ≥ 2.2×GBW×2π×CL

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Calculate key op-amp parameters from given transistor sizing.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 5: Two-Stage Op-Amp Analysis

Given two-stage CMOS op-amp:
**Stage 1 (Diff Pair):**
- M1,M2: NMOS, W/L=50, ISS=200μA, λn=0.05V⁻¹
- M3,M4: PMOS active load, W/L=25, λp=0.1V⁻¹

**Stage 2:**
- M6: NMOS, W/L=200, ID6=500μA, λn=0.05V⁻¹
- M7: PMOS, W/L=100, λp=0.1V⁻¹ (same ID6)

**Compensation:** Cc=3pF, CL=10pF
μnCox=200μA/V², μpCox=100μA/V²

**Calculate:**
1. gm1 (Stage 1 diff pair, ID=ISS/2=100μA)
2. Rout1 = ro2||ro4
3. A1 = gm1 × Rout1
4. gm6 (Stage 2, ID6=500μA)
5. Rout2 = ro6||ro7
6. A2 = gm6 × Rout2
7. Total gain A0 = A1 × A2 (in dB)
8. GBW = gm1/(2π×Cc)
9. Dominant pole frequency fp1
10. Slew rate SR = ISS/Cc
lab.solutionExplanation: Stage1: gm1=1.414mA/V, Rout1=66.7kΩ, A1=94V/V. Stage2: gm6=6.32mA/V, Rout2=13.3kΩ, A2=84V/V. Total A0=7921≈78dB. GBW=75MHz. SR=200μA/3pF=66.7V/μs. These are typical values for a real CMOS op-amp.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your op-amp design knowledge.

quiz.q: Miller compensation capacitor Cc ensures stability by:
options: Creating dominant pole — pushes other poles to higher frequency

quiz.q: GBW (Gain-Bandwidth Product) = gm1/(2π×Cc) means:
options: Increasing gm1 increases GBW while Cc stays same

quiz.q: Slew rate SR = ISS/Cc is limited by:
options: Tail current divided by compensation capacitor

quiz.q: Phase margin must be > 45° because:
options: Less PM means ringing or oscillation in closed-loop response

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Noise in analog circuits — thermal noise, flicker noise, and noise figure.
keyPoints: Thermal noise: v²n = 4kTRΔf (white noise, all frequencies)
Flicker (1/f) noise: S(f) = Kf/(f×Cox×WL) (decreases with frequency)
MOSFET drain thermal noise: i²nd = 4kTγgm
γ = 2/3 for long channel NMOS in saturation
Input-referred noise: divide all noise by amplifier gain²
SNR: Signal-to-Noise Ratio — want as high as possible
Noise figure NF: how much SNR degrades through amplifier
subLevels.content: Noise limits the minimum detectable signal in any analog system. Thermal noise arises from random thermal motion of electrons — it's white (flat spectrum) and unavoidable. Flicker noise (1/f noise) arises from carrier trapping at oxide interfaces and dominates at low frequencies. In CMOS analog design, large transistors reduce 1/f noise (larger WL), while bias current and sizing trade noise against power. The input-referred noise voltage of a MOSFET amplifier is vn_in = √(8kTγ/(3gm)) for the dominant thermal noise term.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: Noise equations for resistors, MOSFETs, and amplifier chains.
keyPoints: Resistor thermal noise: Sv = 4kTR (V²/Hz), Si = 4kT/R (A²/Hz)
MOSFET drain thermal noise: Sid = 4kT×(2/3)×gm
MOSFET 1/f noise: S1f = Kf/(Cox×W×L×f)
Input-referred noise voltage: Svin = Sid/gm² = 4kT×(2/3)/gm
Total input noise: Vn,rms = √(Svin × BW)
Friis formula: NFtotal = NF1 + (NF2-1)/G1 (first stage dominates)
Corner frequency: f_c where 1/f = thermal noise

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Calculate noise figure of a two-stage amplifier using Friis formula.
keyPoints: Friis formula: NF_total = NF1 + (NF2-1)/G1
First stage dominates total noise figure
High first-stage gain suppresses second-stage noise contribution
Input matching: maximize power transfer to reduce NF
Trade-off: high gain first stage may limit linearity

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Calculate input-referred noise and SNR for a sensor interface amplifier.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 6: Noise Analysis

A sensor interface amplifier has:
- Input NMOS transistor: gm=2mA/V, γ=2/3
- Load resistor: RL=10kΩ
- Bandwidth: BW=1MHz
- T=300K, k=1.38×10⁻²³ J/K
- Signal amplitude: Vsig=100μV (peak)

**Tasks:**
1. Input-referred thermal noise PSD from MOSFET: Svin_MOS = 4kT(2/3)/gm
2. Input-referred thermal noise PSD from RL: Svin_RL = 4kT×RL/Av² where Av=gm×RL
3. Total input noise PSD: Svin_total = Svin_MOS + Svin_RL  
4. RMS input noise voltage: Vn_rms = √(Svin_total × BW)
5. SNR = 20×log10(Vsig_rms / Vn_rms) in dB
   where Vsig_rms = Vsig/√2
lab.solutionExplanation: MOSFET dominates input noise at 2.35nV/√Hz. RL contributes only 0.64nV/√Hz after input-referral (load noise is suppressed by Av²=400). Total Vn_rms=2.44μV. With 100μV peak signal (70.7μV rms), SNR=29.2dB. To improve: increase gm (quadruples current or doubles W/L for 3dB improvement).

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your noise analysis knowledge.

quiz.q: Thermal noise of a resistor scales with:
Flicker (1/f) noise is reduced by:
Friis formula shows that in a cascade:
options: First stage noise dominates total NF

quiz.q: To halve input-referred thermal noise of MOSFET:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: ADC and DAC fundamentals — resolution, speed, and architecture trade-offs.
keyPoints: ADC: converts analog voltage to N-bit digital code
Resolution: 1 LSB = Vref/(2^N) — voltage per digital step
INL: Integral Non-Linearity — deviation from ideal transfer curve
DNL: Differential Non-Linearity — deviation in step size
SNR (ideal N-bit ADC) = 6.02×N + 1.76 dB
ENOB: Effective Number of Bits — accounts for real-world non-idealities
Speed-resolution trade-off: flash ADC is fastest but uses 2^N comparators
subLevels.content: ADCs are in every sensing system — they convert the analog world (temperature, pressure, audio, RF signals) into digital numbers for processing. The key specifications are: resolution (N bits → 2^N levels), sampling rate (samples per second), SNR, and power consumption. Different architectures optimize different parameters: Flash ADC is fastest (single clock) but uses 2^N-1 comparators (256 for 8-bit). SAR ADC is efficient (N clocks for N bits) and dominates IoT/sensor applications. Sigma-delta ADC achieves high resolution by oversampling and noise shaping.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: ADC/DAC performance metrics and architecture comparisons.
keyPoints: LSB = Vref / 2^N  (voltage resolution)
Ideal SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 dB (N-bit ADC)
ENOB = (SNR_measured - 1.76) / 6.02
SFDR: Spurious-Free Dynamic Range — largest spur below fundamental
Nyquist: fsample ≥ 2 × fsignal_max
Flash: 1 clock, 2^N-1 comparators — area limited
SAR: N clocks, 1 comparator — area efficient, moderate speed

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through a SAR ADC conversion step by step.
keyPoints: SAR = Successive Approximation Register
Binary search algorithm in hardware
N steps for N-bit result
Step 1: Set MSB=1, compare → if Vin>Vref/2, keep; else clear
Each step halves the search range
Final register = digital representation of Vin

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Design ADC specifications and perform SAR conversion trace.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 7: ADC Design and Analysis

**Part A: ADC Specification**
For an audio ADC (CD quality: 16-bit, 44.1kSps):
1. Calculate 1 LSB voltage (Vref=3.3V)
2. Calculate ideal SNR in dB
3. Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist)
4. If measured SNR = 92dB, find ENOB

**Part B: SAR Conversion**
Trace a 3-bit SAR ADC conversion:
- Vref = 8V, Vin = 5.5V
- LSB = Vref/2^3 = 1V

Complete the binary search table:
| Step | Bit | DAC | Compare | Decision |
|------|-----|-----|---------|----------|
|  1   |  B2 | 4V  | 5.5>4?  | KEEP B2=1 |
|  2   |  B1 | ?V  | 5.5>?   | ??? |
|  3   |  B0 | ?V  | 5.5>?   | ??? |

Final digital code and quantization error.
hints: Step2: DAC=4+2=6V, 5.5>6? NO → B1=0, DAC_current stays at 4V
Code=101 binary=5 decimal; Vout=5×1V=5V; Error=0.5V=0.5 LSB
lab.solutionExplanation: Part A: LSB=50.4μV, SNR_ideal=98.1dB, ENOB=15bits (audio quality excellent). Part B: SAR trace: B2=1(4V<5.5), B1=0(6V>5.5), B0=1(5V<5.5) → code=101=5. Vout=5V, quantization error=0.5V=0.5LSB (always <1 LSB for ideal ADC).

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your ADC/DAC knowledge.

quiz.q: Ideal SNR of a 10-bit ADC:
SAR ADC requires N clock cycles for N bits because:
options: It performs binary search — one bit decided per cycle

quiz.q: Maximum quantization error of an ideal ADC:
ENOB < N means:
options: Real-world non-idealities reduce effective resolution below N bits

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Phase-Locked Loops — frequency synthesis, clock recovery, and jitter.
keyPoints: PLL: feedback system that locks output phase to input reference
Components: PFD, Charge Pump, Loop Filter, VCO, Divider
PFD: Phase-Frequency Detector — compares phases of two clocks
VCO: Voltage-Controlled Oscillator — fout = fvco0 + Kvco×Vc
Frequency multiplication: N = fout/fref (divider ratio)
Lock range: range of fref over which PLL can acquire lock
Jitter: phase noise of output clock — limits system performance
subLevels.content: A PLL is a feedback control system that synchronizes an output oscillator to an input reference. The PFD detects phase/frequency error between reference and divided output. The charge pump converts phase error to current pulses. The loop filter integrates these to create a smooth control voltage for the VCO. The VCO generates the high-frequency output. A frequency divider by N multiplies the reference frequency by N at the output. PLLs are in every chip for clock generation, frequency synthesis in cellular radio, and clock recovery in SerDes.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: PLL loop dynamics, VCO gain, and loop filter design.
keyPoints: VCO: ωout = ω0 + Kvco × Vc (Kvco in rad/s/V)
Open-loop gain: G(s) = Icp×Kvco×Z(s) / (N×s)
Loop bandwidth ωn: determines lock time and jitter filtering
Damping factor ζ: affects overshoot (target ζ = 0.7)
Phase noise: L(Δf) = 10log(S_phase(Δf)/2) in dBc/Hz
VCO phase noise: ≈ -20dB/decade outside loop bandwidth
Reference spur: at fref offset — caused by charge pump mismatch

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a PLL loop filter for target bandwidth and phase margin.
keyPoints: Target: fn=1MHz, PM=60°, fref=10MHz, N=100, fout=1GHz
Step 1: Choose ωn = 2π×fn = 6.28 Mrad/s
Step 2: Size R and C from ωn equation
Step 3: Verify phase margin from loop filter zero
Step 4: Check lock time = ~4/ωn
Step 5: Verify loop BW < fref/10 (stability rule)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Calculate PLL frequency multiplication and estimate lock time.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 8: PLL Design Analysis

A PLL for 2.4GHz Wi-Fi:
- Reference: fref = 40MHz
- Required output: fout = 2400MHz
- VCO gain: Kvco = 500 MHz/V
- Charge pump: Icp = 100μA
- Loop filter: R=10kΩ, C1=500fF
- Divider: N = fout/fref

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate divider ratio N
2. Calculate loop natural frequency ωn using:
   ωn = √(Icp × Kvco_rad / (N × C1))
   where Kvco_rad = 2π × Kvco
3. Calculate lock time ≈ settling_time = 4/ωn
4. Loop bandwidth fn = ωn/(2π)
5. Verify fn < fref/10 (stability rule)
6. Zero frequency fz = 1/(2πRC1). Is fz < fn?
hints: Need to reduce Icp or increase C1 for stable design
lab.solutionExplanation: N=60. ωn=102Mrad/s giving fn=16.2MHz. But fref/10=4MHz stability rule is violated! The design is unstable. Fix: reduce Icp to 10μA or increase C1 to 5pF. This exercise shows why PLL stability analysis is critical — blindly choosing components can lead to unstable designs.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your PLL knowledge.

quiz.q: PLL divider ratio N determines:
VCO gain Kvco is expressed in:
options: Hz/V — frequency change per volt of control voltage

quiz.q: PLL loop bandwidth should be:
Phase noise of a PLL output is dominated by VCO outside loop bandwidth and:
options: Reference/PFD noise inside the loop bandwidth

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: LDO regulators and bandgap references — essential power management blocks.
keyPoints: LDO: Low Dropout Regulator — provides stable Vout from varying Vin
Dropout voltage: Vdo = Vin_min - Vout (must be > 0)
PSRR: Power Supply Rejection Ratio — how well regulator rejects input ripple
Bandgap reference: generates temperature-independent ~1.25V voltage
PTAT: Proportional To Absolute Temperature (positive TC)
CTAT: Complementary To Absolute Temperature (negative TC)
Bandgap cancels PTAT+CTAT: Vref ≈ VBE + α×VT×ln(n) = 1.25V
subLevels.content: LDO regulators are in every mixed-signal chip — they isolate sensitive analog circuits from noisy digital supply. The key specs are: dropout voltage (how close Vin can get to Vout), load regulation (how much Vout changes with load current), and PSRR (how much supply noise appears at output). Bandgap references generate a precise voltage that's independent of temperature and supply — they're used as the reference for LDOs, ADCs, and DACs. The key insight: VBE has -2mV/°C temperature coefficient (CTAT), while the thermal voltage VT=kT/q has +0.085mV/°C (PTAT). Summing them in the right proportion cancels temperature dependence.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: LDO design equations, PSRR, and bandgap reference.
keyPoints: LDO feedback: Vout = Vref × (1 + R1/R2)
PSRR ≈ -20log(gmp×ro_pass / (gm_error×Rout)) dB
Load regulation: ΔVout/ΔIload = 1/(gm_pass × loop_gain)
Bandgap Vref = VBE0 + (kT/q) × α × ln(n) ≈ 1.25V
TC = dVref/dT ≈ 0 at target temperature
VBE temperature coefficient: ≈ -2mV/°C
LDO stability: dominant pole at output with CL and ESR

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a complete LDO regulator — sizing and stability analysis.
keyPoints: Pass transistor: PMOS for low dropout (VGS instead of VSD)
Size pass transistor for max load current with minimum VGS
Error amplifier: drives pass FET gate, provides loop gain
Stability: output capacitor creates dominant pole
ESR zero: capacitor ESR adds stabilizing zero
PSRR: error amp gain × ro_pass (limited by bandwidth)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Design LDO feedback network and verify stability conditions.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 9: LDO Regulator Design

Design an LDO regulator:
- Vin = 3.3V (±10% variation: 2.97V to 3.63V)
- Target Vout = 2.5V
- Vref = 1.2V (bandgap)
- Max load: Iload = 100mA
- Min load: Iload = 1mA
- PMOS pass transistor: μpCox=50μA/V², |VTP|=0.5V

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate R1/R2 ratio for Vout=2.5V
   (Vout = Vref × (1 + R1/R2))
2. Choose R2=200kΩ, find R1
3. Minimum Vin for regulation (Vin_min = Vout + Vdo)
   where Vdo = |VOD_pass|. Assume |VOD_pass|=0.3V.
4. Size PMOS pass transistor (W/L) for Iload=100mA with |VOD|=0.3V
5. Output resistance at max load: Rout = Vout/Iload
6. Dominant pole frequency with CL=4.7μF: fp = 1/(2π×Rout×CL)
lab.solutionExplanation: R1/R2=1.083 sets Vout=2.5V. R1≈217kΩ. Vin_min=2.8V safely met by 2.97V min supply. Pass transistor W/L=44,444 (typical for high-current LDOs). Output pole at 1.35kHz — very low frequency dominant pole ensures stability but limits PSRR bandwidth.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your power management knowledge.

quiz.q: LDO dropout voltage is:
Bandgap reference achieves ~1.25V because:
options: PTAT + CTAT voltages cancel temperature dependence at this voltage

quiz.q: PSRR of LDO represents:
options: How much input supply noise appears at regulated output (want high rejection)

quiz.q: LDO pass transistor must have very large W/L because:
options: It must carry full load current with small VGS overdrive (low dropout)

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Analog layout — matching, parasitic awareness, and common-centroid.
keyPoints: Mismatch: random variation in W, L, VT between transistors on same die
Pelgrom's law: σ(VT) = AVT/√(WL) — larger transistors match better
Common-centroid: layout technique to cancel linear gradients
Interdigitation: alternating fingers of matched transistors
Dummy transistors: at edges to equalize environment
Well proximity effect: VT shifts near well boundary
Metal density rules: ensure uniform CMP planarization
subLevels.content: Analog layout is as important as circuit design — a perfect schematic with poor layout can perform worse than a modest design with careful layout. Mismatch between transistors causes offset in diff pairs, current mirror error, and DAC nonlinearity. The main causes: random dopant fluctuations (quantum), lithographic variations (systematic), and mechanical stress. Common-centroid placement cancels linear and quadratic gradients. Large transistors match better (Pelgrom's law) but cost area and increase capacitance — designer must balance.

subLevels.title: Key Equations
subLevels.summary: Mismatch equations, Pelgrom model, and common-centroid design rules.
keyPoints: σ(ΔVGS) = AVT/√(WL) — threshold mismatch (Pelgrom)
σ(ΔID/ID) = 2×σ(ΔVGS)/VOD (current mismatch from VT mismatch)
Also: σ(ΔID/ID) = Aβ/√(WL) (from mobility/Cox mismatch)
Total: σ(ΔID/ID) ≈ √((2AVT/VOD)² + Aβ²) / √(WL)
Target mismatch: < 0.1% for DAC, < 1% for mirror, < 5mV offset for diff pair
Common-centroid requires: symmetric placement around centroid
Rule: number of fingers per transistor must be even for CC

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design common-centroid layout for a differential pair.
keyPoints: Diff pair: M1 and M2 must be matched
Each transistor split into 2 fingers: M1a, M1b and M2a, M2b
Common-centroid arrangement: M2a | M1a | M1b | M2b
This cancels linear gradient across the array
Add dummy transistors on each end
All fingers same orientation, same metal routing length

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Calculate mismatch and determine required transistor sizing for spec.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 10: Mismatch Analysis and Sizing

**Technology:** AVT=4mV·μm, Aβ=1.5%·μm

**Circuit:** 10-bit current DAC unit cell (current mirror)
- Required current mismatch: σ(ΔI/I) < 0.05% (10-bit accuracy)
- Bias current per unit cell: ID=10μA
- VOD = 0.2V

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate required WL from current mismatch spec:
   σ(ΔI/I) = √((2AVT/VOD)² + Aβ²) / √(WL) < 0.05%

2. Find minimum WL satisfying the spec

3. If L=1μm, find required W

4. Calculate actual σ(ΔVT) and σ(ΔI/I) for your sizing

5. What is the 3-sigma worst case ΔI/I?

6. For a differential pair with 5mV offset spec, find required WL
   (use σ(ΔVT) = AVT/√(WL) < 5mV/3 for 3-sigma)
hints: W = 7310μm with L=1μm (very large! Use shorter L)
lab.solutionExplanation: For 10-bit DAC (0.05% accuracy): WL_min=7310μm² → W=7310μm with L=1μm. This is enormous — in practice use multiple fingers or longer L. For diff pair (5mV/3=1.67mV 1-sigma): WL=5.76μm² (much smaller). The difference shows why precision current sources need far more area than voltage amplifiers.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your analog layout knowledge.

quiz.q: Pelgrom's law states σ(ΔVT) ∝:
Common-centroid layout cancels:
options: Linear and gradient variations across the die

quiz.q: Why are dummy transistors placed at array edges?
options: To equalize etch and implant environment for all active transistors

quiz.q: For a 10-bit DAC unit cell, mismatch requirement is approximately:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What analog IC interviews at top companies actually test.
keyPoints: MOSFET analysis: draw IV curves, identify region, calculate gm, ro
Amplifier design: common-source, cascode, diff pair gain calculations
Feedback theory: closed-loop gain, stability, phase margin
Current mirror: sizing, accuracy, output impedance
Op-amp design: two-stage topology, compensation, GBW
Companies: TI, Analog Devices, Qualcomm, MediaTek, Apple analog team
Most common: 'Design an op-amp meeting these specs on the whiteboard'
subLevels.content: Analog IC interviews are the most technically demanding in the semiconductor industry. You will be asked to analyze circuits from first principles, derive transfer functions, and design circuits to specifications — all on a whiteboard. You must know MOSFET operating regions cold, be able to draw small-signal models instantly, and reason about noise, stability, and PVT variation. Companies like TI, ADI, and Qualcomm will give you a circuit and ask 'what's wrong with this?' or 'how would you improve this for lower power?'

subLevels.title: Common Interview Patterns
subLevels.summary: Top 10 analog circuit analysis patterns asked in every interview.
keyPoints: 1. ID, gm, ro from given W/L and ID
2. CS amplifier gain with and without degeneration
3. Cascode amplifier — gain and output impedance
4. Diff pair — common mode vs differential gain
5. Current mirror — ratio and output impedance
6. Two-stage op-amp — GBW and phase margin
7. Feedback: identify topology, find closed-loop gain
8. PSRR: how does supply noise affect output?
9. Noise: which transistor dominates?
10. Stability: where are poles and zeros?

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Solve 5 classic analog interview questions.
keyPoints: Q1: Find the gain of a CS amp with resistive degeneration
Q2: Why is cascode better than CS for high frequency?
Q3: How does feedback improve op-amp performance?
Q4: What limits the GBW of a two-stage op-amp?
Q5: Design a 1mA current mirror with 1% accuracy

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Classic analog interview — design a complete single-stage amplifier.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 11: Classic Interview — Complete Amplifier Design

Design a single-stage CMOS cascode amplifier:

**Specification:**
- Gain: |Av| ≥ 100 V/V
- Supply: VDD = 1.8V
- Power: P ≤ 1mW
- Load: RD = 10kΩ (resistive)
- μnCox = 200μA/V², λn = 0.05V⁻¹

**Tasks:**
1. Max total current from power budget: I_total = P/VDD
2. Choose ID = 0.4×I_total for main transistor M1 (bottom CS)
3. Calculate required gm for gain: |Av| = gm×RD (use cascode Rout ≈ gm×ro×ro)
4. Actually: for resistive load RD, Av = gm×(RD||Rout_cascode)
   Since Rout_cascode >> RD: Av ≈ gm1×RD
   Find required gm1 = Av/RD
5. Find W/L of M1 from gm and ID: W/L = gm²/(2×μnCox×ID)
6. Calculate VOD: VOD = 2×ID/gm
7. Verify output swing: Vout_max = VDD - ID×RD, Vout_min = VOD_M1 + VOD_M2 ≈ 2×VOD
hints: Need to reduce RD or ID: ID×RD must be < VDD → ID < 180μA with RD=10kΩ
lab.solutionExplanation: The design reveals a constraint violation: ID×RD = 222μA×10kΩ = 2.22V > VDD=1.8V! The transistor would be in triode, not saturation. Fix: reduce ID to 100μA (ID×RD=1V), then gm = 100/10kΩ = 10mA/V, VOD = 20mV (very small — hard to bias stably). Better: use active load instead of resistive RD, or redesign. This is the kind of constraint discovery that good interview answers reveal.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Analog interview-style questions.

quiz.q: Source degeneration in CS amplifier:
options: Reduces gain but improves bandwidth and linearity

quiz.q: Cascode amplifier eliminates Miller effect because:
options: M2 presents low impedance at M1 drain — Cgd signal swing is small

quiz.q: Most common analog IC interview question:
options: Design an op-amp to spec on whiteboard

quiz.q: Shunt feedback at output reduces:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Complete analog IC system design — the final boss challenge.
keyPoints: Sensor interface chain: sensor → LNA → filter → ADC → digital
Signal chain design: allocate noise, gain, dynamic range at each stage
Power-noise trade-off: lower power → higher noise → poorer SNR
PVT robustness: design must work across all corners
Calibration: digital correction of analog imperfections
Mixed-signal co-design: analog and digital must be co-optimized
Chip-level integration: substrate coupling, power supply integrity
subLevels.content: Complete analog IC system design requires integrating all building blocks into a coherent signal chain. A typical IoT sensor interface: the sensor generates microvolts; a low-noise amplifier (LNA) boosts the signal; an anti-aliasing filter removes high-frequency noise; an ADC digitizes the signal. Each stage must be designed with awareness of: how much gain is needed, what noise is acceptable, what dynamic range is required, and how PVT variation affects each stage. The total noise referred to the input is the root-sum-square of all stage contributions (dominated by first stage per Friis).

subLevels.title: System Architecture
subLevels.summary: Complete signal chain budget analysis — gain, noise, dynamic range.
keyPoints: SNR budget: distribute across stages for optimal power efficiency
Gain budget: each stage gain multiplied by remaining chain gain
Noise budget: input-referred noise = noise1 + noise2/G1² + noise3/(G1G2)²
Dynamic range: DR = 20log(Vfs/Vn_rms) where Vfs=full scale
SFDR: determines harmonic distortion floor
Power distribution: most power to first stage for noise, last for drive
Area budget: matching transistors dominate area for precision blocks

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Complete two-stage op-amp design meeting all specs.
keyPoints: Spec: GBW=50MHz, PM=65°, CL=2pF, SR≥100V/μs, Av>70dB
Step 1: Cc from SR: Cc=ISS/SR
Step 2: gm1 from GBW
Step 3: Size stage 1 transistors
Step 4: Stage 2 gm6 for phase margin
Step 5: Verify all specs

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: FINAL BOSS — Design a complete two-stage op-amp meeting all specifications.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 12: FINAL BOSS — Complete Op-Amp Design

## Specification
| Parameter | Min | Typ | Max | Unit |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|------|
| DC Gain A0 | 70 | 80 | — | dB |
| GBW | 20 | — | — | MHz |
| Phase Margin | 60 | 65 | — | ° |
| Slew Rate | 50 | — | — | V/μs |
| CL (given) | — | 5 | — | pF |
| VDD | — | 1.8 | — | V |
| Power | — | — | 500 | μW |

**Process:** μnCox=200μA/V², μpCox=100μA/V², λn=0.05V⁻¹, λp=0.1V⁻¹

## Design Steps
1. **Power budget**: I_total = P/VDD; allocate ISS and ID6
2. **Choose Cc**: Cc = ISS/SR (from slew rate)
3. **Calculate gm1**: gm1 = GBW×2π×Cc
4. **Size M1/M2 (PMOS diff pair)**: W/L from gm1 and ID1=ISS/2
5. **Size M6 for PM=60°**: gm6/CL ≥ 2.2×GBW×2π
6. **ID6 from gm6**: ID6 = gm6×VOD6/2 (choose VOD6=0.3V)
7. **Calculate Rout1, Rout2, A1, A2, A0**
8. **Verify A0 ≥ 70dB (3162 V/V)**
9. **If not meeting gain, propose fix**

**Show complete design with all values.**
hints: 65.3dB < 70dB → need to double L: λ/2 → ro×2 → A0+6dB=71.3dB ✓
lab.solutionExplanation: Complete design: ISS=83μA, Cc=1.66pF, gm1=208μA/V, WL1_p=5.2. Stage1: A1=33.5V/V. gm6=1.38mA/V, A2=55V/V. Total A0=1842=65.3dB — just below 70dB spec. Fix: double L from 1μm to 2μm → λ halves → ro doubles → A0≈71dB. This is exactly how real op-amp design works: iterate to close the spec. Congratulations — you've completed the Analog IC design path!

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Final boss — complete analog architect knowledge.

quiz.q: In a sensor interface chain, noise is dominated by:
options: First stage (LNA) — all later stage noise divided by first stage gain²

quiz.q: If two-stage op-amp gain is below spec, best fix is:
options: Double channel length L (halves λ, doubles ro, +6dB gain)

quiz.q: PVT corners for analog design means:
Analog Architect role requires:
options: System-level partitioning + circuit design + layout awareness + verification across PVT
